figures.caption: Fig. 1: High-level overview of Muninn applied to Diffuser. Diffuser recomputes the denoiser at every diffusion step, while Muninn wraps the same model and selectively reuses cached denoiser outputs at some steps, reducing compute while leaving the overall trajectory generation unchanged.

TABLE I: Offline RL and trajectory planning. Task metric is the benchmark metric (D4RL normalized score for D4RL tasks; success rate for Kuka stacking). 𝑑 ( ·, · ) is the trajectory deviation metric used for risk accounting and ?̂?viol = P ( 𝑑 (𝜏full 0 , ?̃?0 ) > 𝜂traj ) .

TABLE XI: Probe and cache-eligibility ablations (suite-level aggregates) Top: Shallow stem probes provide the best net acceleration (low probe overhead with strong predictiveness). Deeper probes increase correlation but reduce net speedup due to higher probe cost; extremely shallow probes (LN(input)) are cheap but weakly predictive, forcing conservative reuse. Bottom: Forbidding reuse in a small prefix/suffix (here 𝑘=5 and 2𝑘=10 steps) reduces collisions/violations with a modest speedup reduction, consistent with avoiding the most sensitive regions of the diffusion chain.
TABLE XV: Diffuser (Full) training details used in our runs

TABLE XIV: Temporal U-Net denoiser used by trajectory diffusion planners (our runs) This is the canonical backbone used by Diffuser-style planners in our evaluation: 1D temporal convolutions, GroupNorm, Mish nonlinearity, and additive injection of timestep/context embeddings inside each residual block.
TABLE III: Visuomotor imitation and manipulation. Latency is measured per policy inference/control call. 𝑑 ( ·, · ) is defined on the trajectory segment.
TABLE XXVI: DP3 (Full) training details used in our runs
TABLE XXV: DP3 architecture used in our runs
TABLE XXIV: Diffusion Policy (Full) training details used in our runs

TABLE XXVIII: Probe instantiations used for every evaluated model For each architecture family we define a concrete stem/core split and a deterministic pooling rule. The probe is computed on every diffusion step; the core is executed only on recompute steps

TABLE XII: Runtime escalation ablation on hardware (150 runs) We report end-to-end planning/policy latency per call (including Muninn probe and sampling), task success rate, and collision/violation rate. Escalation rate and resampling rate are computed over planning/policy calls during closed-loop execution (not episodes). Full-override rates (calls with 𝜌 > 𝜌full): ASV (both planners) 1.0%, UAV 1.0%, SO-ARM100 2.0%.
TABLE XXI: MPD (Full) training details used in our runs
TABLE XXIII: Diffusion Policy architecture used in our runs RLBench uses the vision encoder; Meta-World pick-place-v2 uses state-only conditioning (no image encoder)
TABLE XX: MPD (Full) denoiser architecture used in our runs
TABLE XXII: EDMP (Full) details used in our runs EDMP differs from MPD primarily in its inference-time ensemble guidance; the learned diffusion prior is trained identically

TABLE X: Extended ablations (suite-level aggregates) (i) Sweeping 𝜀traj yields a predictable speed–fidelity trade-off (higher budgets permit more reuse, decreasing Evals/𝑇 and increasing speedup). (ii) Risk calibration across target levels 𝛼 summarized by realized violation probability ?̂?viol and MACE; split-conformal calibration tracks the target risk more reliably than heuristic alternatives. (iii) Sensitivity-aware budgeting improves the speed– safety trade-off; removing {𝐿𝑡 } increases collisions/violations and raises ?̂?viol at comparable compute. (vi) Calibration is sample-efficient; beyond a modest number of rollouts, both speedup and ?̂?viol stabilize.
TABLE XVI: Decision Diffuser (Full) training details used in our runs
TABLE XIX: CompDiff (Full) training details used in our runs
TABLE XVIII: AdaptDiff (Full) training details used in our runs
TABLE XVII: Diffusion-QL (Full) training details Learning-rate / 𝜂 settings follow the published Diffusion-QL per-task grid; we use the inference step counts 𝑇eval reported in Table I

Fig. 5: (Stages 1–3) From offline rollouts, we build a calibration set of score–error pairs by running the full planner with a ghost reuse chain. (Stage 4) A regressor 𝑚(𝑠) models the typical score–error relationship, and split conformal prediction yields 𝑈 (𝑠) . At test time, Muninn maps each score to 𝑈 (𝑠𝑡 ) , converts it to a trajectory-level cost, and spends it from the budget.
